    column_names.push_back(field.name());
  }
  
  std::string sql = "INSERT INTO " + table_name + " (";
  for (size_t i = 0; i < column_names.size(); ++i) {
    sql += column_names[i];
    if (i < column_names.size() - 1) sql += ", ";
  }
  sql += ") VALUES (";
  for (size_t i = 0; i < column_names.size(); ++i) {
    sql += "?";
    if (i < column_names.size() - 1) sql += ", ";
  }
  sql += ")";
  
  for (const auto& record : records) {
    std::vector<sqlgen::Value> params;
    rfl::for_each_field(record, [&](const auto& field) {
      params.push_back(sqlgen::Value{field});
    });
    
    sqlgen::execute(conn, sql, params.begin(), params.end()).value();
  }
}

void modify_eas_records_by_id(const std::string& table_name,
                              const int record_id,
                              const std::string& col_name,
                              const std::string& new_value) {
  static const std::map<std::string, std::string> primary_keys = {
    {"Student", "SNo"},
    {"Course", "CID"},
    {"Score", "ScID"},
    {"Major", "MID"}
  };
  
  auto it = primary_keys.find(table_name);
  if (it == primary_keys.end()) {
    throw std::runtime_error("Unknown table: " + table_name);
  }
  
  std::string sql = "UPDATE " + table_name + " SET " + col_name + " = ? WHERE " + it->second + " = ?";
  std::vector<sqlgen::Value> params = {new_value, record_id};
  
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path).value();
  sqlgen::execute(conn, sql, params.begin(), params.end()).value();
}

void delete_eas_records_by_id(const std::string& table_name,
                              const std::string& record_id) {
  static const std::map<std::string, std::string> primary_keys = {
    {"Student", "SNo"},
    {"Course", "CID"},
    {"Score", "ScID"},
    {"Major", "MID"}
  };
  
  auto it = primary_keys.find(table_name);
  if (it == primary_keys.end()) {
    throw std::runtime_error("Unknown table: " + table_name);
  }
  
  std::string sql = "DELETE FROM " + table_name + " WHERE " + it->second + " = ?";
  std::vector<sqlgen::Value> params = {record_id};
  
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path).value();
  sqlgen::execute(conn, sql, params.begin(), params.end()).value();
}
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace delete_inactive_users {

struct User {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string username;
  std::optional<sqlgen::DateTime> last_login;
  std::string status = "active";
};

int delete_inactive_users() {
  try {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    conn.begin_transaction();
    
    // Execute the DELETE statement
    conn.execute("DELETE FROM User WHERE last_login < DATE('2025-01-01')");
    
    // Commit the transaction and return the result
    return conn.commit() ? 0 : 1;
  } catch (const std::exception& e) {
    // Return -1 on any exception
    return -1;
  }
}

} // namespace delete_inactive_users
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect` to establish a connection to "test_db.sqlite"
2. **Transaction Safety**: Wraps the deletion operation in a transaction using `begin_transaction()` and `commit()`
3. **Conditional Deletion**: Executes the SQL statement to delete users who haven't logged in since 2025-01-01
4. **Error Handling**: Uses try-catch to handle potential exceptions and returns appropriate status codes
5. **Return Value**: Returns 0 on successful commit, 1 if commit fails, and -1 on any exception during execution</answer>

#pragma once

    rfl::Field<"Remark", std::optional<std::string>> Remark;

    rfl::Field<"CTime", std::optional<int>> CTime;
    rfl::Field<"Credit", float> Credit = 0.0f;

  };

  struct Score {
    rfl::Field<"SNo", std::string> SNo;
    rfl::Field<"CID", int> CID;
    rfl::Field<"Score", std::optional<float>> Score;
    
    rfl::PrimaryKey<SNo, CID>;
    rfl::ForeignKey<Student, "SNo"> SNo_fk;
    rfl::ForeignKey<Course, "CID"> CID_fk;
  };

    using namespace sqlgen;
    using namespace sqlgen::literals;

        .and_then(drop<Student> | if_exists)
        .and_then(drop<Course> | if_exists)
        .and_then(drop<Score> | if_exists)
        .and_then(create_table<Student> | verbose)
        .and_then(create_table<Course> | verbose)
        .and_then(create_table<Score> | verbose);

  }

} // namespace test_design_eas_database

#pragma once

#include <optional>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <chrono>
#include <iomanip>
#include <sstream>

namespace query_active_users_posts {

struct User {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string username;
};

struct Post {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> post_id;
  int user_id;
  sqlgen::DateTime created_at;
};

struct ActiveUser {
  static constexpr const char* tablename = "ActiveUserView";
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string username;
};

std::vector<ActiveUser> query_active_users_posts() {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Create tables
    conn.create_table<User>();
    conn.create_table<Post>();
    
    // Execute query to find active users
    auto active_users = conn.and_then([&](auto& conn) {
        return conn.select_from<User>()
                   .inner_join<Post, User::user_id, Post::user_id>()
                   .where(Post::created_at >= "2025-12-01"_t && Post::created_at <= "2025-12-31"_t)
                   .distinct()
                   .transform([](const auto& user) {
                       return ActiveUser{user.user_id, user.username};
                   });
    }).value();
    
    return active_users;
}

}  // namespace query_active_users_posts

Here's the complete code:

```cpp
#pragma once

#include <optional>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace query_course_enrollment_stats {

struct Course {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> course_id;
  std::string title;
};

struct Enrollment {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> enrollment_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Course, "course_id"> course_id;
  std::optional<float> grade;
};

struct CourseStats {
  std::string title;
  int student_count;
  std::optional<double> avg_grade;
};

std::vector<CourseStats> query_course_enrollment_stats() {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;

  // Connect to the SQLite database
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");

  // Create Course and Enrollment tables
  auto create_course = sqlgen::write<Course>();
  auto create_enrollment = sqlgen::write<Enrollment>();

  // Execute the query to get enrollment statistics
  auto query_result = conn.and_then(create_course)
                         .and_then(create_enrollment)
                         .and_then([](auto conn) {
                             // Execute the SQL query
                             return conn.execute(
                                 "SELECT c.title, COUNT(e.enrollment_id) as student_count, AVG(e.grade) as avg_grade "
                                 "FROM Course c "
                                 "LEFT JOIN Enrollment e ON c.course_id = e.course_id "
                                 "GROUP BY c.course_id, c.title;"
                             );
                         })
                         .and_then([](auto&& rows) -> std::vector<CourseStats> {
                             std::vector<CourseStats> stats;
                             
                             for (const auto& row : rows) {
                                 CourseStats cs;
                                 cs.title = row.get<std::string>(0);
                                 cs.student_count = row.get<int>(1);
                                 
                                 // Handle average grade (could be NULL)
                                 if (row.has_value(2)) {
                                     cs.avg_grade = row.get<double>(2);
                                 } else {
                                     cs.avg_grade = std::nullopt;
                                 }
                                 
                                 stats.push_back(std::move(cs));
                             }
                             
                             return stats;
                         });

  // Return the result (handle potential errors)
  if (query_result) {
    return *query_result;
  } else {
    return {}; // Return empty vector on error
  }
}

}  // namespace query_course_enrollment_stats
```

This code connects to the database, creates the necessary tables, executes the SQL query to get the enrollment statistics, and processes the results into a vector of CourseStats. The solution handles the optional average grade correctly and uses sqlgen's functionality to interact with the SQLite database.

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <ranges>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace query_customer_purchase_history {

struct Customer {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> customer_id;
  std::string name;
  std::string email;
};

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;
};

struct Order {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Customer, "customer_id"> customer_id;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
  double total_amount;
};

struct OrderItem {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> item_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Order, "order_id"> order_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Product, "product_id"> product_id;
  int quantity;
};

struct PurchaseHistory {
  std::string product_name;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
  int quantity;
};

std::vector<PurchaseHistory> query_customer_purchase_history(int customer_id) {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    auto result = conn.execute(
        R"(SELECT p.name, o.order_date, oi.quantity FROM Customer c JOIN "Order" o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id JOIN OrderItem oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id JOIN Product p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id WHERE c.customer_id = ? ORDER BY o.order_date DESC)",
        customer_id
    );
    
    std::vector<PurchaseHistory> purchases;
    for (auto& row : result) {
        PurchaseHistory ph;
        ph.product_name = row.get<std::string>("name");
        ph.order_date = row.get<sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S">>("order_date");
        ph.quantity = row.get<int>("quantity");
        purchases.push_back(ph);
    }
    
    return purchases;
}
}  // namespace query_customer_purchase_history
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <ranges>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace query_daily_traffic_metrics {

struct PageVisit {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> visit_id;
  std::string url;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> visit_time;
};

struct DailyTraffic {
  std::string visit_date;
  int total_visits;
  int unique_pages;
};

struct DailyTrafficView {
  static constexpr const char* tablename = "DailyTrafficView";
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  std::string visit_date;
  int total_visits;
  int unique_pages;
};

/**
 * Function: Calculate daily traffic metrics from a log table.
 * 
 * @return List of daily traffic metrics
 */
std::vector<DailyTraffic> query_daily_traffic_metrics() {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;

  auto conn = sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  std::string sql = R"(
    SELECT strftime('%Y-%m-%d', visit_time) as visit_date, 
           COUNT(*) as total_visits, 
           COUNT(DISTINCT url) as unique_pages
    FROM PageVisit
    GROUP BY visit_date
    ORDER BY visit_date
  )";

  return conn.and_then([&](auto&& conn) {
    return sqlite::read<std::vector<DailyTraffic>>(conn, sql);
  }).value_or(std::vector<DailyTraffic>{});
}

}  // namespace query_daily_traffic_metrics
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")` to establish a connection to the SQLite database.

2. **SQL Query**: Executes a raw SQL query that:
   - Extracts the date from `visit_time` using `strftime`
   - Groups records by the extracted date
   - Calculates total visits (`COUNT(*)`) and unique pages (`COUNT(DISTINCT url)`)
   - Orders results chronologically

3. **Result Mapping**: Uses `sqlite::read<std::vector<DailyTraffic>>` to automatically map the SQL result set to C++ structs based on matching field names.

4. **Error Handling**: Uses `value_or` to return an empty vector if the query fails.

The solution efficiently leverages sqlgen's capabilities for database operations while maintaining clean, readable code that directly maps to the required business logic.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace query_high_value_orders {

struct Customer {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> customer_id;
  std::string name;
};

  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  int customer_id;
  double total_amount;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
};

struct OrderView {
  static constexpr const char* tablename = "HighValueOrderView";
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  std::string name;
  double total_amount;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
};

std::vector<OrderView> query_high_value_orders() {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  return conn.read<std::vector<OrderView>>(
    R"(SELECT o.order_id, c.name, o.total_amount, o.order_date
       FROM "Order" o
       INNER JOIN Customer c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
       WHERE o.total_amount > 1000
       ORDER BY o.total_amount DESC)"
  );
}

}  // namespace query_high_value_orders
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace query_product_sales_report {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string category;
};

struct Sale {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> sale_id;
  int product_id;
  int quantity;
  double amount;
};

struct CategoryReport {
  std::string category;
  int total_qty;
  double total_amt;
};

std::vector<CategoryReport> query_product_sales_report() {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    auto session = sqlgen::make_session(conn);
    
    return sqlgen::read<std::vector<CategoryReport>>(session,
        R"(SELECT p.category, SUM(s.quantity) as total_qty, SUM(s.amount) as total_amt
           FROM Product p
           INNER JOIN Sale s ON p.product_id = s.product_id
           GROUP BY p.category
           HAVING total_amt > 5000)");
}

}  // namespace query_product_sales_report
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <optional>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace query_search_books_by_criteria {

struct Book {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> book_id;
  std::string title;
  std::string author;
  int year;
  std::string genre;
  double price;
};

struct BookFilters {
  std::optional<std::string> title_keyword;
  std::optional<int> min_year;
  std::optional<std::string> genre;
  std::optional<double> max_price;
};

std::vector<Book> query_search_books_by_criteria(const BookFilters& filters) {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("sqlite:test_db.sqlite");
    
    auto query = sqlgen::select_from<Book>();
    
    if (filters.title_keyword) {
        query.where(query.title.like("%" + *filters.title_keyword + "%"));
    }
    
    if (filters.min_year) {
        query.where(query.year >= *filters.min_year);
    }
    
    if (filters.genre) {
        query.where(query.genre == *filters.genre);
    }
    
    if (filters.max_price) {
        query.where(query.price <= *filters.max_price);
    }
    
    return conn.and_then([&](auto session) {
        return session.execute(query).and_then(
            [](auto cursor) { return sqlgen::to_vector<Book>(cursor); }
        );
    }).value();
}

}  // namespace query_search_books_by_criteria
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Connects to `test_db.sqlite` using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()`
2. **Dynamic Query Construction**: 
   - Starts with `select_from<Book>()` as the base query
   - Conditionally adds `WHERE` clauses using `where()` method only when filters have values
   - Uses `LIKE` with wildcards for title search
   - Uses comparison operators (`>=`, `=`, `<=`) for year, genre, and price filters
3. **Query Execution**: 
   - Executes the constructed query through the database session
   - Converts the result cursor to a vector of `Book` objects using `sqlgen::to_vector()`
4. **Error Handling**: Uses `and_then()` for monadic error handling and extracts the value with `.value()`

The solution efficiently handles optional filters by building the query dynamically while maintaining type safety and proper SQL injection prevention through parameterized queries.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace schema_create_trigger_audit_log {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;
  int stock;
};

struct AuditLog {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int, sqlgen::auto_incr> log_id;
  std::string table_name;
  int record_id;
  std::string action;
  sqlgen::DateTime timestamp;
};

/**
 * Function: Create a trigger for auditing changes on Product table.
 * 
 * @return true if trigger creation succeeds, false otherwise
 */
bool create_trigger_audit_log() {
  try {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    std::string trigger_sql = R"(
      CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS audit_product_update
      AFTER UPDATE ON Product
      BEGIN
          INSERT INTO AuditLog (table_name, record_id, action, timestamp)
          VALUES ('Product', OLD.product_id, 'UPDATE', CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);
      END;
    )";
    
    sqlgen::exec(conn, trigger_sql);
    return true;
  } catch (...) {
    return false;
  }
}

}  // namespace schema_create_trigger_audit_log
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace transaction_transfer_funds {

struct Account {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> account_id;
  double balance;
};

bool transfer_funds(int from_account_id, int to_account_id, double amount) {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    try {
        // Start transaction
        conn.execute("BEGIN TRANSACTION;");
        
        // Deduct from sender (only if sufficient funds)
        auto result1 = conn.execute(
            "UPDATE Account SET balance = balance - ? WHERE account_id = ? AND balance >= ?",
            amount, from_account_id, amount
        );
        
        // Check if sender account exists and has sufficient funds
        if (result1.affected_rows() != 1) {
            conn.execute("ROLLBACK;");
            return false;
        }
        
        // Add to receiver
        auto result2 = conn.execute(
            "UPDATE Account SET balance = balance + ? WHERE account_id = ?",
            amount, to_account_id
        );
        
        // Check if receiver account exists
        if (result2.affected_rows() != 1) {
            conn.execute("ROLLBACK;");
            return false;
        }
        
        // Commit transaction if both updates succeeded
        conn.execute("COMMIT;");
        return true;
        
    } catch (const std::exception& e) {
        // Rollback on any exception
        conn.execute("ROLLBACK;");
        return false;
    }
}

}  // namespace transaction_transfer_funds
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
// eas_common.hpp
#pragma once

#include <filesystem>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

using namespace sqlgen::literals;

struct Student {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<sqlgen::Varchar<10>> SNo;
  sqlgen::Varchar<30> SName;
  int Sex = 1;
  std::optional<sqlgen::DateTime> Birthday;
  std::optional<uint32_t> Major;
  std::optional<double> Scorses = 0.0;
  std::optional<std::string> Remark;
};

struct Course {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<uint32_t, sqlgen::auto_incr> CID;
  sqlgen::Varchar<50> CName;
  std::optional<int> Term = 1;
  std::optional<int> CTime;
  int Credit = 0;
};

struct Score {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<uint32_t, sqlgen::auto_incr> ScID;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<sqlgen::Varchar<10>, Student, "SNo"> SNo;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<uint32_t, Course, "CID"> CID;
  std::optional<double> Score;
};

struct Major {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<uint32_t> MID;
  sqlgen::Varchar<30> MName;
};

const std::string db_path = "data/eas_database.db";

void design_eas_database_without_major() {
  std::filesystem::create_directories("data");
  sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path)
      .and_then([](auto&& conn) {
        return sqlgen::exec(conn, "PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;");
      })
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Student> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Course> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Score> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .value();
}

void design_eas_database_with_major() {
  design_eas_database_without_major();

  sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path)
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Major> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .value();
}

// update_eas_table.hpp
#pragma once

#include <filesystem>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

#include "eas_common.hpp"

namespace update_eas_table {

void update_eas_table() {
  design_eas_database_with_major();
  
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path).value();
  
  // Task 1: Add Grade column to Score table
  sqlgen::exec(conn, "ALTER TABLE Score ADD COLUMN Grade CHAR(1) NOT NULL;");
  
  // Task 2: Modify Age column in Student table (SQLite limitation noted)
  sqlgen::exec(conn, "ALTER TABLE Student MODIFY Age INT NULL;");
  
  // Task 3: Drop Major table
  sqlgen::exec(conn, "DROP TABLE Major;");
}

} // namespace update_eas_table
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Add Grade Column**: Uses raw SQL `ALTER TABLE` to add the required column with constraints
2. **Modify Age Column**: Uses raw SQL `ALTER TABLE` (note: this may not work as expected in SQLite due to its limited ALTER TABLE support)
3. **Drop Major Table**: Uses raw SQL `DROP TABLE` to remove the table

The solution uses `sqlgen::exec()` for all operations since the specific schema modifications aren't directly mappable to the struct definitions and require raw SQL execution. The code handles SQLite's connection management and error propagation through the `sqlgen` library's monadic interface.</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <optional>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

namespace upsert_user_profile {

struct UserProfile {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::optional<std::string> bio;
  std::optional<std::string> avatar_url;
};

bool upsert_user_profile(const UserProfile& u) {
    try {
        auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
        conn.execute(
            "INSERT OR REPLACE INTO UserProfile (user_id, bio, avatar_url) VALUES (?, ?, ?)",
            {u.user_id, u.bio, u.avatar_url}
        );
        return true;
    } catch (const std::exception& e) {
        return false;
    }
}

} // namespace upsert_user_profile

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <ranges>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <gtest/gtest.h>

namespace schema_create_view_sales_summary {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;
  int stock;
};

struct Sale {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int, sqlgen::auto_incr> sale_id;
  int product_id;
  int quantity;
  double amount;
};

struct SalesSummary {
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  std::string product_name;
  int total_quantity;
  double total_revenue;
};

bool create_view_sales_summary() {
  try {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    std::string query = R"(
      CREATE VIEW SalesSummary AS
      SELECT p.name as product_name, SUM(s.quantity) as total_quantity, SUM(s.amount) as total_revenue
      FROM Sale s
      JOIN Product p ON s.product_id = p.product_id
      GROUP BY p.name
    )";
    
    conn.execute(query);
    return true;
  } catch (const std::exception& e) {
    return false;
  }
}

}  // namespace schema_create_view_sales_summary

// Test case
TEST(sqlite, test_create_view_sales_summary) {
  EXPECT_TRUE(schema_create_view_sales_summary::create_view_sales_summary());
}
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")` to establish a connection to the SQLite database.

2. **View Creation Query**: Constructs a SQL query that:
   - Creates a view named `SalesSummary`
   - Joins `Sale` and `Product` tables on `product_id`
   - Groups results by `product_name`
   - Calculates `total_quantity` and `total_revenue` using aggregate functions

3. **Error Handling**: Wraps the database operation in a try-catch block to handle potential exceptions and return a boolean indicating success/failure.

4. **View Structure**: The `SalesSummary` struct is marked with `is_view = true` to indicate it represents a database view in the `sqlgen` ORM.

5. **Testing**: Includes a Google Test case to verify the view creation functionality.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>
#include <stdexcept>

namespace right_join_departments_projects {

struct Department {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> department_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct Project {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> project_id;
  int department_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct DepProj {
  std::optional<std::string> department_name;
  std::string project_name;
};

std::vector<DepProj> right_join_departments_projects() {
  try {
    // Connect to the SQLite database
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Execute the RIGHT JOIN query
    auto result = conn.and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<DepProj>>(
        R"(SELECT d.name AS department_name, p.name AS project_name "
           "FROM Project p "
           "RIGHT JOIN Department d ON p.department_id = d.department_id)"
    ));
    
    // Check if the query was successful
    if (!result) {
      throw std::runtime_error("Database query failed: " + 
                             std::string(result.error().what()));
    }
    
    return result.value();
  }
  catch (const std::exception& e) {
    // Handle any exceptions that occur during database operations
    throw std::runtime_error("Error in right_join_departments_projects: " + 
                            std::string(e.what()));
  }
}

}  // namespace right_join_departments_projects
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: The function connects to the SQLite database "test_db.sqlite" using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()`.

2. **RIGHT JOIN Query**: The SQL query performs a RIGHT JOIN between the `Project` and `Department` tables. This ensures all departments are included in the results, with project information where available.

3. **Column Selection**: 
   - `d.name AS department_name` - Includes department names (always present due to RIGHT JOIN)
   - `p.name AS project_name` - Includes project names (NULL/empty for departments without projects)

4. **Result Handling**: 
   - Uses `sqlgen::read` to parse the query results into a `std::vector<DepProj>`
   - Includes proper error handling with try-catch blocks
   - Throws descriptive exceptions if the database operation fails

5. **Struct Mapping**: 
   - `department_name` is `std::optional<std::string>` to handle cases where department data might be missing (though RIGHT JOIN guarantees department presence)
   - `project_name` is a regular `std::string` (NULL values from the database become empty strings in C++)

This solution correctly implements the RIGHT JOIN requirement while handling potential database errors and ensuring all departments are included in the results.</answer>

3. Add WHERE conditions: B.score > 10 AND A.name LIKE '%x%'.
4. Order by B.score DESC.
5. Limit to 5 rows.
6. Convert the query to SQL string and return it.

Now, translating this into sqlgen code.

First, the select_from<A>() starts the query. Then, the join with B. The join condition is on the primary keys. Since A has a PrimaryKey<int> id, and B has a_id which is presumably a foreign key to A's id. So the join condition is A.id = B.a_id.

In sqlgen, the join can be specified with sqlgen::join<B>(), and the on condition is created with sqlgen::on().

So:

auto query = sqlgen::select_from<A>()
    | sqlgen::join<B>(sqlgen::on(sqlgen::col<A, &A::id> == sqlgen::col<B, &B::a_id>));

Then, the WHERE clause:

query | sqlgen::where(
    (sqlgen::col<B, &B::score> > 10) && 
    sqlgen::col<A, &A::name>.like("%x%")
);

Then order_by:

query | sqlgen::order_by<sqlgen::col<B, &B::score>, sqlgen::desc>{};

Then limit:

query | sqlgen::limit(5);

Finally, convert to SQL string. But how? The to_sql function might be a member function or a free function. Looking at sqlgen examples, there's a to_sql function that takes a query and returns the string.

So, the complete query building would look like:

auto final_query = sqlgen::select_from<A>()
    | sqlgen::join<B>(sqlgen::on(sqlgen::col<A, &A::id> == sqlgen::col<B, &B::a_id>))
    | sqlgen::where(
        (sqlgen::col<B, &B::score> > 10) && 
        sqlgen::col<A, &A::name>.like("%x%")
    )
    | sqlgen::order_by<sqlgen::col<B, &B::score>, sqlgen::desc>{}
    | sqlgen::limit(5);

Then, to get the SQL string, use to_sql(final_query).

But wait, in the code example provided, the function is supposed to return the SQL string. So the function would return to_sql(final_query).

Putting all this into the function:

std::string generate_sql_dry_run() {
    auto query = sqlgen::select_from<A>()
        | sqlgen::join<B>(sqlgen::on(sqlgen::col<A, &A::id> == sqlgen::col<B, &B::a_id>))
        | sqlgen::where(
            (sqlgen::col<B, &B::score> > 10) && 
            sqlgen::col<A, &A::name>.like("%x%")
        )
        | sqlgen::order_by<sqlgen::col<B, &B::score>, sqlgen::desc>{}
        | sqlgen::limit(5);
    
    return sqlgen::to_sql(query);
}

Wait, but the function is supposed to connect to the SQLite database. However, the dry run doesn't execute the query, just generates the SQL. So maybe the connection part is not needed here. The original code in the problem statement includes the sqlite.hpp header, but the function doesn't actually use it. Unless the to_sql function requires a connection, but I don't think so. The to_sql function probably just serializes the query object to a string.

Therefore, the code should work as written. Let me check if the syntax is correct.

The join syntax: sqlgen::join<B> takes a condition. The condition is created with sqlgen::on(), which takes an equality expression between the columns.

Yes, sqlgen::col<A, &A::id> == sqlgen::col<B, &B::a_id> creates the equality condition.

The where clause uses && to combine the two conditions: score > 10 and name like '%x%'.

The order_by uses the template parameters to specify the column and direction (desc).

The limit is straightforward.

So the code should generate the correct SQL.

Testing the generated SQL: it should have SELECT, FROM A, JOIN B ON A.id = B.a_id, WHERE B.score > 10 AND A.name LIKE '%x%', ORDER BY B.score DESC, LIMIT 5.

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace generate_sql_dry_run {

struct A {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  std::string name;
};

struct B {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  int a_id;
  double score;
};

std::string generate_sql_dry_run() {
    using namespace sqlgen;
    
    // Build the query using sqlgen DSL
    auto query = select_from<A>()
        | join<B>(on(col<A, &A::id> == col<B, &B::a_id>))
        | where((col<B, &B::score> > 10) && col<A, &A::name>.like("%x%"))
        | order_by<col<B, &B::score>, desc>{}
        | limit(5);
    
    // Generate SQL string without execution (dry run)
    return to_sql(query);
}

}  // namespace generate_sql_dry_run
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Query Construction**: Uses sqlgen's fluent interface to build a complex query with:
   - Join between tables A and B on primary key relationship
   - WHERE clause with compound conditions (score > 10 AND name contains 'x')
   - ORDER BY with descending sort on score
   - LIMIT to restrict results

2. **Dry Run Generation**: The `to_sql()` function converts the query object to a SQL string without executing it against the database

3. **Expected Output**: The function returns a SQL string similar to:
   ```sql
   SELECT * FROM A JOIN B ON A.id = B.a_id WHERE (B.score > 10) AND (A.name LIKE '%x%') ORDER BY B.score DESC LIMIT 5
   ```

This solution demonstrates proper use of the sqlgen library for building type-safe SQL queries with compile-time validation of table structures and relationships.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>
#include <optional>

namespace analyze_temporal_data {

struct Event {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> event_id;
  std::string event_name;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> start_time;
  std::optional<sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S">> end_time;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> created_at;
};

struct TemporalAnalysis {
  int event_id;
  std::string event_name;
  int start_day;
  int start_month;
  int start_year;
  int start_hour;
  int start_minute;
  int start_second;
  int start_weekday;
  int64_t start_epoch;
  std::optional<double> duration_days;
};

std::vector<TemporalAnalysis> analyze_temporal_data(const std::vector<Event>& events) {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  auto session = sqlgen::make_session(conn);
  
  // Create a temporary table for the events
  session->execute(R"(
    CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE TempEvent (
      event_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
      event_name TEXT NOT NULL,
      start_time TEXT NOT NULL,
      end_time TEXT,
      created_at TEXT NOT NULL
    )
  )");
  
  // Insert events into the temporary table
  for (const auto& event : events) {
    session->execute(
      "INSERT INTO TempEvent (event_id, event_name, start_time, end_time, created_at) "
      "VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)",
      event.event_id, event.event_name, event.start_time.str(),
      event.end_time ? event.end_time.str() : nullptr, event.created_at.str()
    );
  }
  
  // Execute the query to extract temporal data
  auto result = session->read<std::vector<TemporalAnalysis>>(
    R"(
      SELECT 
        event_id,
        event_name,
        CAST(strftime('%d', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_day,
        CAST(strftime('%m', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_month,
        CAST(strftime('%Y', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_year,
        CAST(strftime('%H', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_hour,
        CAST(strftime('%M', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_minute,
        CAST(strftime('%S', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_second,
        CAST(strftime('%w', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_weekday,
        CAST(strftime('%s', start_time) AS INTEGER) as start_epoch,
        CASE WHEN end_time IS NOT NULL THEN 
          julianday(end_time) - julianday(start_time) 
        ELSE NULL END as duration_days
      FROM TempEvent
      WHERE end_time IS NOT NULL
    )"
  );
  
  return result;
}

}  // namespace analyze_temporal_data
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: The function connects to `test_db.sqlite` using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect`.

2. **Temporary Table Creation**: A temporary table `TempEvent` is created with the same structure as the `Event` struct to isolate the analysis.

3. **Data Insertion**: Events are inserted into the temporary table, handling optional `end_time` values appropriately.

4. **Temporal Analysis Query**: The SQL query extracts:
   - Date components using `strftime` (day, month, year, hour, minute, second)
   - Weekday information using `strftime('%w')`
   - Unix epoch timestamp using `strftime('%s')`
   - Duration calculation using `julianday()` for precise day differences

5. **Result Mapping**: The query results are automatically mapped to the `TemporalAnalysis` struct by `sqlgen::read`.

6. **Isolation**: Using a temporary table ensures the original database remains unchanged after the session ends.

The solution demonstrates comprehensive temporal data analysis using SQLite's built-in date/time functions through the `sqlgen` ORM library.</answer>

#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace limit_recent_events {

struct Event {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> event_id;
  std::string name;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> created_at;
};

/**
 * description: limit_recent_events.
 * This function demonstrates how to limit the number of recent events returned.
 * It orders the events by created_at in descending order and returns the top n events.
 * Returns: std::vector<Event>.
 */
std::vector<Event> limit_recent_events(size_t n = 10) {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then(sqlgen::read<Event> | sqlgen::order_by("created_at", sqlgen::desc) | sqlgen::limit(n))
      .value();
}

}  // namespace limit_recent_events
```</answer>

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace analyze_social_network {

struct User {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string username;
};

struct Friendship {
  int user1_id;
  int user2_id;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> created_at;
};

struct FriendConnection {
  std::string user1_name;
  std::string user2_name;
};

std::vector<FriendConnection> analyze_social_network_impl(const std::vector<User>& users, const std::vector<Friendship>& friendships) {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;

  // Connect to SQLite database
  auto db = sqlite::connect();

  // Create tables
  db.and_then([&](auto& conn) {
    conn.execute("PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;");
    return conn.create_table<User>() && conn.create_table<Friendship>();
  });

  // Insert data
  db.and_then([&](auto& conn) {
    return conn.insert(users) && conn.insert(friendships);
  });

  // Execute the first reference SQL query to find mutual friends
  auto result = db.and_then([&](auto& conn) {
    return conn.execute(R"(
      SELECT u1.username, u2.username
      FROM User u1
      INNER JOIN Friendship f1 ON u1.user_id = f1.user_id1
      INNER JOIN Friendship f2 ON f1.user_id2 = f2.user_id1
      INNER JOIN User u2 ON f2.user_id2 = u2.user_id
      WHERE u1.user_id < u2.user_id
        AND EXISTS (
          SELECT 1 FROM Friendship f3 
          WHERE f3.user_id1 = u1.user_id AND f3.user_id2 = u2.user_id
        )
      GROUP BY u1.user_id, u2.user_id
      HAVING COUNT(f1.user_id2) > 0
    )");
  });

  // Convert results to FriendConnection objects
  std::vector<FriendConnection> connections;
  for (const auto& row : result.value()) {
    connections.push_back(FriendConnection{
      row.get<std::string>(0),
      row.get<std::string>(1)
    });
  }

  return connections;
}

}  // namespace analyze_social_network
```

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace analyze_social_network {

struct User {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string username;
  std::string email;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> join_date;
  std::string city;
};

struct Friendship {
  int user_id1;
  int user_id2;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> created_at;
};

struct FriendConnection {
  std::string user1_name;
  std::string user2_name;
};

std::vector<FriendConnection> analyze_social_network_impl(const std::vector<User>& users, const std::vector<Friendship>& friendships) {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;

  // Connect to SQLite database
  auto db = sqlite::connect();

  // Enable foreign key constraints
  db.and_then([](auto& conn) {
    conn.execute("PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;");
    return true;
  });

  // Create tables
  db.and_then([&](auto& conn) {
    return conn.create_table<User>() && conn.create_table<Friendship>();
  });

  // Insert data
  db.and_then([&](auto& conn) {
    return conn.insert(users) && conn.insert(friendships);
  });

  // Query 1: Find mutual friends (friends who share at least one common friend)
  auto mutual_friends_query = db.and_then([&](auto& conn) {
    return conn.execute(R"(
      SELECT u1.username, u2.username
      FROM User u1
      INNER JOIN Friendship f1 ON u1.user_id = f1.user_id1
      INNER JOIN Friendship f2 ON f1.user_id2 = f2.user_id1
      INNER JOIN User u2 ON f2.user_id2 = u2.user_id
      WHERE u1.user_id < u2.user_id
        AND EXISTS (
          SELECT 1 FROM Friendship f3 
          WHERE f3.user_id1 = u1.user_id AND f3.user_id2 = u2.user_id
        )
      GROUP BY u1.user_id, u2.user_id
      HAVING COUNT(f1.user_id2) > 0
    )");
  });

  // Query 2: Find users with more than average friends
  auto above_avg_friends_query = db.and_then([&](auto& conn) {
    return conn.execute(R"(
      SELECT u.username, COUNT(f.user_id2) as friend_count
      FROM User u
      LEFT JOIN Friendship f ON u.user_id = f.user_id1
      GROUP BY u.user_id, u.username
      HAVING friend_count > (
        SELECT AVG(friend_count) 
        FROM (SELECT COUNT(*) as friend_count FROM Friendship GROUP BY user_id1)
      )
      ORDER BY friend_count DESC
    )");
  });

  // Query 3: Find friend-of-friend relationships (connected through exactly one mutual friend)
  auto friend_of_friend_query = db.and_then([&](auto& conn) {
    return conn.execute(R"(
      SELECT u1.username, u3.username
      FROM User u1
      INNER JOIN Friendship f1 ON u1.user_id = f1.user_id1
      INNER JOIN Friendship f2 ON f1.user_id2 = f2.user_id1
      INNER JOIN Friendship f3 ON f2.user_id2 = f3.user_id1
      INNER JOIN User u3 ON f3.user_id2 = u3.user_id
      WHERE u1.user_id < u3.user_id
        AND NOT EXISTS (
          SELECT 1 FROM Friendship f4 
          WHERE f4.user_id1 = u1.user_id AND f4.user_id2 = u3.user_id
        )
      GROUP BY u1.user_id, u3.user_id
      HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT f2.user_id2) = 1
    )");
  });

  // Query 4: Count friends per user and find most connected users
  auto friend_count_query = db.and_then([&](auto& conn) {
    return conn.execute(R"(
      SELECT u.username, COUNT(f.user_id2) as friend_count
      FROM User u
      LEFT JOIN Friendship f ON u.user_id = f.user_id1
      GROUP BY u.user_id, u.username
      ORDER BY friend_count DESC
      LIMIT 10
    )");
  });

  // Query 5: Recommend users in same city who are not yet friends
  auto recommendation_query = db.and_then([&](auto& conn) {
    return conn.execute(R"(
      SELECT u1.username, u2.username, u1.city
      FROM User u1
      INNER JOIN User u2 ON u1.city = u2.city AND u1.user_id < u2.user_id
      LEFT JOIN Friendship f ON (f.user_id1 = u1.user_id AND f.user_id2 = u2.user_id) 
                         OR (f.user_id1 = u2.user_id AND f.user_id1 = u1.user_id)
      WHERE f.user_id1 IS NULL
      ORDER BY u1.city, RANDOM()
      LIMIT 10
    )");
  });

  // For this implementation, we'll return mutual friends as the primary result
  // In a complete solution, you might return multiple result types or use a more sophisticated return structure
  std::vector<FriendConnection> connections;
  
  // Process mutual friends query results
  for (const auto& row : mutual_friends_query.value()) {
    connections.push_back(FriendConnection{
      row.get<std::string>(0),
      row.get<std::string>(1)
    });
  }

  return connections;
}

}  // namespace analyze_social_network
```

This solution provides a comprehensive implementation that:

1. Connects to an SQLite database and enables foreign key constraints
2. Creates the required `User` and `Friendship` tables
3. Inserts the provided data
4. Executes five different analytical queries covering:
   - Mutual friends analysis using self-joins
   - Above-average friend count analysis using nested queries
   - Friend-of-friend relationships
   - Friend count statistics
   - Recommendation system for same-city users

The solution uses sqlgen's SQLite backend with proper error handling and demonstrates advanced SQL techniques including self-joins, nested queries, aggregation, and complex filtering conditions. The code is structured to be extensible for additional analytical queries while maintaining clean separation of concerns.</answer>

#include <sqlgen.hpp>

#include <vector>

namespace mathematical_operations_analysis {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;

  double discount_rate;
};

struct MathAnalysis {
  int product_id;
  std::string name;
  double abs_price;
  double ceil_price;
  double floor_price;
  double rounded_price;
  double exp_discount;
  double ln_price;
  double log2_quantity;

  double cos_discount;

  double sqrt_quantity;
};

  // Create a temporary table and insert the products

    "TAN(discount_rate) as tan_discount, "
    "SQRT(quantity) as sqrt_quantity "
    "FROM Product"
  ));
  
  // Return the results
  return result.value();
}

}  // namespace mathematical_operations_analysis

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace mathematical_operations_analysis {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;
  int quantity;
  double discount_rate;
};

struct MathAnalysis {
  int product_id;
  std::string name;
  double abs_price;
  double ceil_price;
  double floor_price;
  double rounded_price;
  double exp_discount;
  double ln_price;
  double log2_quantity;
  double sin_discount;
  double cos_discount;
  double tan_discount;
  double sqrt_quantity;
};

std::vector<MathAnalysis> analyze_mathematical_operations(const std::vector<Product>& products) {
  // Connect to the SQLite database file
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  
  // Write products to the database table
  sqlgen::write(conn, "Product", products);
  
  // Execute the mathematical operations query
  auto result = conn.and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<MathAnalysis>>(
    R"(
      SELECT 
        product_id,
        name,
        ABS(price) as abs_price,
        CEIL(price) as ceil_price,
        FLOOR(price) as floor_price,
        ROUND(price, 2) as rounded_price,
        EXP(discount_rate/100) as exp_discount,
        LN(price) as ln_price,
        LOG2(quantity) as log2_quantity,
        SIN(discount_rate) as sin_discount,
        COS(discount_rate) as cos_discount,
        TAN(discount_rate) as tan_discount,
        SQRT(quantity) as sqrt_quantity
      FROM Product
    )"
  ));
  
  // Return the results
  return result.value();
}

}  // namespace mathematical_operations_analysis
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: The function connects to `test_db.sqlite` using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()`.

2. **Data Insertion**: Uses `sqlgen::write()` to create a `Product` table and insert the input products.

3. **Mathematical Operations**: Executes a SQL query that performs:
   - Absolute value (`ABS`)
   - Ceiling (`CEIL`) and floor (`FLOOR`)
   - Rounding (`ROUND`)
   - Exponential (`EXP`)
   - Natural logarithm (`LN`) and base-2 logarithm (`LOG2`)
   - Trigonometric functions (`SIN`, `COS`, `TAN`)
   - Square root (`SQRT`)

4. **Result Mapping**: The query results are automatically mapped to the `MathAnalysis` struct using sqlgen's type inference.

5. **Return Value**: Returns a vector containing the calculated values for each product.

**Note**: This solution assumes the `Product` table is created dynamically. For production use, you would want to add proper error handling and ensure the database schema matches expectations.</answer>

output.reasoning:
<think>Okay, I need to write a function called trim_and_length_validation using the sqlgen library. Let me start by understanding what the problem requires.

First, the function should connect to an SQLite database named "test_db.sqlite". The existing table is UserProfile with columns user_id, name, and email. The task is to trim the name and email fields (using ltrim, rtrim, trim) and compute their lengths. Then return this processed data as a view or a vector of ValidatedProfile structs.

Looking at the provided code structure, there's a UserProfile struct and a ValidatedProfile struct. The ValidatedProfile has user_id, trimmed_name, trimmed_email, name_length, and email_length. So the function needs to query the database, process each row by trimming the name and email, calculate their lengths, and then collect all these into a vector.

Using sqlgen, I remember that you can use the read function to execute SQL queries and map the results to a struct. So the plan is to write a SQL query that trims the name and email, calculates their lengths, and then uses sqlgen::read to convert the result into a vector of ValidatedProfile.

Wait, but how do I perform the trimming in SQL? SQLite has functions like TRIM(), LTRIM(), RTRIM(). So in the SQL query, I can use TRIM(name) to remove leading and trailing spaces from the name, same for email. Then LENGTH() to get the length after trimming.

So the SQL query would be something like:

  double unit_price;
  int quantity;
  std::optional<double> discount_rate;
  std::optional<double> tax_rate;
};

struct OrderMetrics {
  int item_id;
  int order_id;
  int product_id;
  double unit_price;
  int quantity;
  double discount_rate;
  double tax_rate;
  double gross_total;
  double discounted_total;
  double final_total;
  double unit_cost;
  int quantity_group;
  double estimated_profit;
};

std::vector<OrderMetrics> calculate_order_metrics_impl(const std::vector<OrderItem>& items) {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect();
  
  // Create the OrderItem table
  conn.and_then(sqlgen::create_table<OrderItem>);
  
  // Insert all items into the table
  conn.and_then(sqlgen::insert(items));
  
  // Execute the complex SELECT query with arithmetic operations
  auto result = conn.and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<OrderMetrics>> << R"(
    SELECT 
      item_id,
      order_id,
      product_id,
      unit_price,
      quantity,
      COALESCE(discount_rate, 0) as discount_rate,
      COALESCE(tax_rate, 0) as tax_rate,
      (unit_price * quantity) as gross_total,
      (unit_price * quantity * (1 - COALESCE(discount_rate, 0)/100)) as discounted_total,
      (unit_price * quantity * (1 - COALESCE(discount_rate, 0)/100) * (1 + COALESCE(tax_rate, 0)/100)) as final_total,
      (unit_price * quantity / quantity) as unit_cost,
      (quantity % 10) as quantity_group,
      (unit_price * 0.3) as estimated_profit
    FROM OrderItem
  )").value();
  
  return result;
}

}  // namespace calculate_order_metrics
```

This solution demonstrates:
1. Database connection and table creation using `sqlgen`
2. Arithmetic operations in SQL including multiplication, division, and modulo
3. Handling of optional values with `COALESCE`
4. Complex business metric calculations
5. Proper type mapping between SQL results and C++ structs

The function processes order items through SQL operations to calculate comprehensive business metrics including totals, discounts, taxes, unit costs, quantity groupings, and estimated profits.</answer>

#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace join_orders_customers_payments {

struct Customer {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> customer_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct Order {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  int customer_id;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
};

struct Payment {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> payment_id;
  int order_id;
  double amount;
};

struct Consolidated {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  std::string customer_name;
  double amount;
};

std::vector<Consolidated> join_orders_customers_payments() {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then([](auto conn) {
        return conn.select_from<Customer>()
            .inner_join<Order>("customer_id")
            .inner_join<Payment>("order_id")
            .select("order_id", "name as customer_name", "amount")
            .order_by("amount", sqlgen::desc)
            .transform([](auto&& data) {
              std::vector<Consolidated> result;
              for (const auto& row : data) {
                result.push_back(Consolidated{
                  row.order_id,
                  row.customer_name,
                  row.amount
                });
              }
              return result;
            });
      })
      .value();
}

}  // namespace join_orders_customers_payments
```

3. Selects the required columns with proper aliasing

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace replace_text_in_notes {

struct Note {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> note_id;
  std::string content;
};

struct UpdatedNote {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> note_id;
  std::string updated_content;
};

std::vector<UpdatedNote> replace_text_in_notes() {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  
  // Replace all occurrences of "foo" with "bar" in the content column
  conn.update<Note>()
      .set("content", "REPLACE(content, 'foo', 'bar')")
      .execute();
  
  // Retrieve all updated notes
  auto notes = conn.select_from<Note>().where(sqlgen::is_valid).execute();
  
  // Convert to UpdatedNote format
  std::vector<UpdatedNote> result;
  for (const auto& note : notes) {
    result.push_back(UpdatedNote{
        note.note_id,
        note.content  // Now contains the replaced text
    });
  }
  
  return result;
}

}  // namespace replace_text_in_notes
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Establishes a connection to `test_db.sqlite` using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()`.

2. **Text Replacement**: Uses SQLite's built-in `REPLACE()` function to substitute all occurrences of "foo" with "bar" in the `content` column of the `Notes` table.

3. **Update Execution**: The `update<Note>()` method targets the `Notes` table, and `.set("content", "REPLACE(...)")` specifies the replacement operation.

4. **Data Retrieval**: After updating, the function queries all valid records using `select_from<Note>().where(sqlgen::is_valid)` to ensure we get the most recent data.

5. **Result Formatting**: Converts the retrieved `Note` objects into the required `UpdatedNote` format before returning.

This solution efficiently handles the text replacement operation while maintaining data integrity through proper database transaction handling inherent in the `sqlgen` library.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <functional>

namespace inventory_bulk_write_and_read {

struct Inventory {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> item_id;
  std::string name;
  int quantity;
  std::string location;
};

bool inventory_bulk_write_and_read(const std::vector<Inventory>& items) {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;

  return sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then(drop<Inventory> | if_exists)
      .and_then(create_as<Inventory, Overwrite>)
      .and_then(write(items))
      .and_then([](auto&& conn) {
        return conn.and_then(
            select_from<Inventory> |
            where([](const Inventory& inv) { return inv.quantity > 50; }) |
            order_by(&Inventory::quantity, std::greater<>{}) |
            to<std::vector<Inventory>>()
        );
      })
      .and_then([](const auto& results) { return !results.empty(); })
      .value();
}

}  // namespace inventory_bulk_write_and_read
```

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlite::connect` to establish connection to "test_db.sqlite"
2. **Table Management**: Drops existing table (if exists) and recreates it with `Overwrite`
3. **Bulk Write**: Uses `write(items)` for efficient batch insertion
4. **Filtered Read**: Applies `where` clause to filter records with `quantity > 50`
5. **Ordering**: Uses `order_by` with `std::greater<>` for descending sort
6. **Result Handling**: Checks if returned results are non-empty before returning

The solution correctly handles the SQLite operations while maintaining type safety and proper error handling through the `sqlgen` monadic interface.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace cascade_delete_demo {

struct Parent {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> parent_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct Child {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> child_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Parent, "parent_id"> parent_id;
  std::string note;
};

bool cascade_delete_demo() {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;

  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
    .and_then([](auto& db) { return db.execute("PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON"); })  // Enable foreign key constraints
    .and_then([](auto& db) { return db.execute("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS Child"); })
    .and_then([](auto& db) { return db.execute("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS Parent"); })
    .and_then([](auto& db) { return db.create_table<Parent>(); })
    .and_then([](auto& db) { return db.create_table<Child>(); })
    .and_then([](auto& db) { return db.insert(Parent{1, "test_parent"}); })
    .and_then([](auto& db) { return db.insert(Child{1, 1, "child_note_1"}); })
    .and_then([](auto& db) { return db.insert(Child{2, 1, "child_note_2"}); })
    .and_then([](auto& db) { return db.execute("DELETE FROM Parent WHERE parent_id = 1"); })
    .and_then([](auto& db) { 
      return db.read<std::vector<Child>>("SELECT * FROM Child WHERE parent_id = 1");
    })
    .transform([](const auto& children) { return children.empty(); });

  return conn.value();
}

}  // namespace cascade_delete_demo

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace update_set_operations {

struct Account {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> account_id;
  double balance;
  std::string status;
};

bool update_set_operations() {
  try {
    // Connect to SQLite database
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Create Account table
    sqlgen::write::create_table<Account>(conn);
    
    // Insert initial data
    std::vector<Account> initial_accounts = {
      Account{1, 500.0, "inactive"},
      Account{2, 750.0, "pending"}
    };
    sqlgen::write::insert(conn, initial_accounts);
    
    // Update account 1: set balance to 1000.0 and status to "active"
    sqlgen::write::update<Account>(conn,
      sqlgen::where("account_id" == 1),
      sqlgen::set("balance" = 1000.0, "status" = "active")
    );
    
    // Update all accounts with status "pending" to "active"
    sqlgen::write::update<Account>(conn,
      sqlgen::where("status" == "pending"),
      sqlgen::set("status" = "active")
    );
    
    // Verify changes by reading all accounts
    auto result = sqlgen::read<std::vector<Account>>(conn);
    
    // Check if updates were applied correctly
    if (result.size() != 2) return false;
    
    // Verify account 1 was updated
    if (result[0].account_id != 1 || result[0].balance != 1000.0 || result[0].status != "active") {
      return false;
    }
    
    // Verify account 2 was updated
    if (result[1].account_id != 2 || result[1].balance != 750.0 || result[1].status != "active") {
      return false;
    }
    
    return true;
    
  } catch (const std::exception& e) {
    // Return false on any error
    return false;
  }
}

}  // namespace update_set_operations
```

This solution demonstrates:
1. Database connection and table creation
2. Initial data insertion
3. Multiple update operations using SET clauses
4. Verification of persisted changes
5. Proper error handling with try-catch
6. Return value indicating operation success/failure

The code uses sqlgen's fluent interface for building update queries with conditions and SET operations, ensuring the changes are properly committed and verified.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>

namespace full_join_suppliers_products {

struct Supplier {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> supplier_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  int supplier_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct SupProd {
  std::string supplier_name;
  std::optional<std::string> product_name;
};

std::vector<SupProd> full_join_suppliers_products() {
  const auto query = R"(
    SELECT s.name as supplier_name, p.name as product_name
    FROM Supplier s
    FULL JOIN Product p ON s.supplier_id = p.supplier_id
  )";

  auto db = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  return db.and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<SupProd>>(query)).value();
}
}  // namespace full_join_suppliers_products
```

This solution uses the `sqlgen` library to perform a FULL JOIN between the Supplier and Product tables, mapping the results to the SupProd struct. The function handles database connection and query execution while properly managing optional product names.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>

namespace analyze_department_employees {

struct Department {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> department_id;
  std::string department_name;
  std::string location;
};

struct Employee {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> employee_id;
  int department_id;
  std::string first_name;
  std::string last_name;
  double salary;
};

struct JoinResult {
  std::optional<std::string> department_name;
  std::optional<std::string> first_name;
  std::optional<std::string> last_name;
  std::optional<double> salary;
};

struct JoinAnalysis {
  std::vector<JoinResult> inner_join_results;
  std::vector<JoinResult> left_join_results;
  std::vector<JoinResult> right_join_results;
  std::vector<JoinResult> full_join_results;
};

JoinAnalysis analyze_department_employees_impl(const std::vector<Department>& depts, const std::vector<Employee>& emps) {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect();

  // Create temporary tables and insert data
  conn.and_then([&](auto db) {
    db.execute("CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE Department (department_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, department_name TEXT, location TEXT)");
    db.execute("CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE Employee (employee_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, department_id INTEGER, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT, salary REAL)");

    for (const auto& dept : depts) {
      db.execute("INSERT INTO Department VALUES (?, ?, ?)", 
                 dept.department_id, dept.department_name, dept.location);
    }

    for (const auto& emp : emps) {
      db.execute("INSERT INTO Employee VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)", 
                 emp.employee_id, emp.department_id, emp.first_name, emp.last_name, emp.salary);
    }
    
    return db;
  });

  // Execute INNER JOIN
  auto inner_results = conn.and_then([&](auto db) {
    std::vector<JoinResult> results;
    auto query = db.execute("SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary FROM Department d INNER JOIN Employee e ON d.department_id = e.department_id");
    
    for (const auto& row : query) {
      JoinResult jr;
      jr.department_name = row.get<std::string>("department_name");
      jr.first_name = row.get<std::string>("first_name");
      jr.last_name = row.get<std::string>("last_name");
      jr.salary = row.get<double>("salary");
      results.push_back(jr);
    }
    return results;
  });

  // Execute LEFT JOIN
  auto left_results = conn.and_then([&](auto db) {
    std::vector<JoinResult> results;
    auto query = db.execute("SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary FROM Employee e LEFT JOIN Department d ON e.department_id = d.department_id");
    
    for (const auto& row : query) {
      JoinResult jr;
      jr.department_name = row.get<std::string>("department_name");
      jr.first_name = row.get<std::string>("first_name");
      jr.last_name = row.get<std::string>("last_name");
      jr.salary = row.get<double>("salary");
      results.push_back(jr);
    }
    return results;
  });

  // Execute RIGHT JOIN (simulated with LEFT JOIN on reversed tables)
  auto right_results = conn.and_then([&](auto db) {
    std::vector<JoinResult> results;
    auto query = db.execute("SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary FROM Department d LEFT JOIN Employee e ON d.department_id = e.department_id");
    
    for (const auto& row : query) {
      JoinResult jr;
      jr.department_name = row.get<std::string>("department_name");
      jr.first_name = row.get<std::string>("first_name");
      jr.last_name = row.get<std::string>("last_name");
      jr.salary = row.get<double>("salary");
      results.push_back(jr);
    }
    return results;
  });

  // Execute FULL JOIN (simulated with UNION of LEFT and RIGHT joins)
  auto full_results = conn.and_then([&](auto db) {
    std::vector<JoinResult> results;
    auto query = db.execute(
      "SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary "
      "FROM Employee e LEFT JOIN Department d ON e.department_id = d.department_id "
      "UNION "
      "SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary "
      "FROM Department d LEFT JOIN Employee e ON d.department_id = e.department_id"
    );
    
    for (const auto& row : query) {
      JoinResult jr;
      jr.department_name = row.get<std::string>("department_name");
      jr.first_name = row.get<std::string>("first_name");
      jr.last_name = row.get<std::string>("last_name");
      jr.salary = row.get<double>("salary");
      results.push_back(jr);
    }
    return results;
  });

  // Compile results
  JoinAnalysis analysis;
  analysis.inner_join_results = std::move(inner_results.value());
  analysis.left_join_results = std::move(left_results.value());
  analysis.right_join_results = std::move(right_results.value());
  analysis.full_join_results = std::move(full_results.value());

  return analysis;
}

}  // namespace analyze_department_employees
```

This solution provides a comprehensive implementation that:
1. Creates temporary tables for Departments and Employees
2. Inserts the provided data into these tables
3. Executes four different types of joins (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL)
4. Handles NULL values using std::optional
5. Returns a structured analysis of all join results

The solution properly handles SQLite's limitations by simulating RIGHT and FULL joins using LEFT joins and UNION operations.</answer>

#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace delete_with_complex_condition {

struct User {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  int age;
  std::string status;
};

/**

 */
inline std::string delete_with_complex_condition() {
    auto query = sqlgen::delete_from<User>()

}

}  // namespace delete_with_complex_condition

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>
#include <optional>

namespace coalesce_function {

struct Profile {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  std::optional<std::string> nickname;
};

struct NicknameResult {
  std::string display_name;
};

/**
 * This function demonstrates COALESCE usage.
 * Returns: query value.
 */
inline auto coalesce_function() {
    return sqlgen::read<Profile> |
           sqlgen::select_from<Profile, NicknameResult>(
               "SELECT COALESCE(nickname, 'Anonymous') AS display_name FROM profiles"
           );
}

}  // namespace coalesce_function
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Struct Definitions:**
   - `Profile`: Represents the data model with an `id` (primary key) and an optional `nickname`.
   - `NicknameResult`: The result structure containing the `display_name` field.

2. **Query Construction:**
   - Uses `sqlgen::read<Profile>` to read from the `profiles` table.
   - Applies `sqlgen::select_from` with a custom SQL query that uses `COALESCE(nickname, 'Anonymous')` to handle null values.
   - The SQL query directly maps to the `NicknameResult` structure via the `display_name` alias.

3. **Return Value:**
   - Returns a query object that can be executed to retrieve the results, where null nicknames are replaced with `'Anonymous'` using the database's `COALESCE` function.</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

namespace create_index_basic {

struct Book {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  std::string title;
};

/**
 * This function demonstrates creating an index.
 */
inline auto create_index_basic() {
    return sqlgen::create_index<sqlgen::Index<"idx_book_title">, Book, sqlgen::Column<Book, &Book::title>>;
}

}  // namespace create_index_basic
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Book Struct Definition**: The `Book` struct is defined with an auto-incrementing primary key (`id`) and a `title` field.

2. **Index Creation**: The `create_index_basic` function uses sqlgen's type-safe DSL to create a CREATE INDEX statement:
   - `sqlgen::Index<"idx_book_title">` specifies the index name as a template parameter
   - `Book` indicates the table we're creating the index on
   - `sqlgen::Column<Book, &Book::title>` references the specific column (`title`) in the `Book` table

3. **Type Safety**: The solution leverages C++'s type system to ensure the index is created on the correct table and column at compile time.

4. **Return Value**: The function returns a `sqlgen::Statement` object that can be executed against a database connection or converted to a SQL string using `sqlgen::to_sql()`.

This solution provides a robust, type-safe way to create database indexes while maintaining the flexibility and power of the sqlgen library.</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace get_top_products {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string product_name;
  std::string category;
  double price;
};

struct Sales {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> sale_id;
  int product_id;
  int quantity;
  sqlgen::Date sale_date;
};

struct TopProduct {
  int product_id;
  std::string product_name;
  std::string category;
  double total_sales;
};

/**
 * Retrieves the top products based on total sales.
 * @param limit_count Number of top products to return (default: 10).
 * @return Vector containing top products by sales.
 */
std::vector<TopProduct> get_top_products(size_t limit_count = 10) {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  
  std::string sql = R"(SELECT p.product_id, p.product_name, p.category, SUM(p.price * s.quantity) as total_sales 
                        FROM Product p 
                        INNER JOIN Sales s ON p.product_id = s.product_id 
                        GROUP BY p.product_id, p.product_name, p.category 
                        ORDER BY total_sales DESC

#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>

namespace update_inventory_stock {

bool update_inventory_stock(const std::unordered_map<int, int>& received) {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;
  
  // Connect to database
  auto conn_res = sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  if (!conn_res) return false;

  auto txn_res = begin_transaction(conn_res.value());
  if (!txn_res) return false;

  // Update each product's stock within transaction
  for (const auto& [product_id, quantity] : received) {
    auto update_result = sqlgen::update("Product")
                         .set("stock", "stock + ?", quantity)
                         .where("product_id = ?", product_id)
                         .exec(txn);
    
    if (!update_result) {
      rollback(txn);
      return false;
    }
  }
  
  // Commit transaction
  auto commit_result = commit(txn);
  if (!commit_result) {
    rollback(txn);
    return false;
  }
  
  return true;
}

}  // namespace update_inventory_stock
```

**Key Improvements:**
1. **Removed unnecessary table creation** - The problem assumes the table already exists
2. **Parameterized queries** - Uses `?` placeholders to prevent SQL injection and handle types correctly
3. **Proper transaction handling** - Implements rollback on any failure during updates or commit
4. **RAII-like transaction management** - Ensures database consistency through explicit commit/rollback

The solution ensures atomic updates and handles errors gracefully while maintaining database integrity.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace create_view_order_summary {

struct Order {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  std::string customer;
};

struct OrderItem {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> item_id;
  int order_id;
  double unit_price;
  int quantity;
};

struct OrderSummary {
  static constexpr const char* tablename = "OrderSummaryView";
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  std::string customer;
  double total;
};

std::vector<OrderSummary> create_view_order_summary() {
    using namespace sqlgen;
    using namespace sqlgen::literals;

    // Connect to the SQLite database
    auto conn = sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");

    // Create the view
    conn.and_then([&](auto& c) {
        c.execute(R"(
            CREATE VIEW OrderSummaryView AS
            SELECT o.order_id, o.customer, SUM(oi.unit_price * oi.quantity) as total
            FROM Order o
            INNER JOIN OrderItem oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
            GROUP BY o.order_id, o.customer
        )");
    });

    // Read from the view and return the results
    return sqlgen::read<std::vector<OrderSummary>>(conn).value();
}

}  // namespace create_view_order_summary
```

This solution:
1. Connects to the SQLite database "test_db.sqlite"
2. Executes the SQL statement to create the required view
3. Reads from the view using the `OrderSummary` struct mapping
4. Returns the aggregated order summary data as a vector
5. Uses proper error handling through monadic operations
6. Leverages the `sqlgen` library's type-safe database operations</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace weekday_sales_analysis {

struct Sales {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> sale_id;
  int product_id;
  int quantity;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> sale_time;
};

struct WeekdayStats {
  int weekday;
  int total_quantity;
};

std::vector<WeekdayStats> weekday_sales_analysis() {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then([](auto conn) {
        return sqlgen::read<std::vector<WeekdayStats>>(conn,
            sqlgen::select_from<Sales>{
                sqlgen::columns{
                    sqlgen::cast(sqlgen::strftime("%w", &Sales::sale_time), sqlgen::types::Integer).as("weekday"),
                    sqlgen::sum(&Sales::quantity).as("total_quantity")
                },
                sqlgen::group_by("weekday"),
                sqlgen::order_by("weekday")
            }
        );
      })
      .value();
}
}  // namespace weekday_sales_analysis
```

This solution:
1. Connects to the SQLite database using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect`
2. Uses SQL's `strftime("%w", sale_time)` to extract weekday (0=Sunday to 6=Saturday)
3. Groups sales by weekday and calculates total quantity using `SUM(quantity)`
4. Returns results ordered by weekday number
5. Handles potential errors through `and_then()` chaining
6. Uses type-safe column references through member pointers
7. Properly casts the weekday string to integer for correct grouping</answer>

#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

namespace update_with_arithmetic {

struct Player {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  int score;
};

    return sqlgen::update<Player>().set("score", "score + 10").to_sql();

}  // namespace update_with_arithmetic

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace project_time_tracking {

struct Project {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> project_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct TimeLog {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> log_id;
  int project_id;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> start_time;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> end_time;
};

struct TimeMetrics {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> project_id;
  std::string name;
  int day;
  int month;
  int year;
  int hour;
  int minute;
  int second;
  double duration_days;
};

std::vector<TimeMetrics> project_time_tracking() {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  
  return sqlgen::read<std::vector<TimeMetrics>>(conn, 
    R"(SELECT p.project_id, p.name,
       t.start_time.day as day, t.start_time.month as month, t.start_time.year as year,
       t.start_time.hour as hour, t.start_time.minute as minute, t.start_time.second as second,
       t.days_between(t.start_time, t.end_time) as duration_days
    FROM Project p
    INNER JOIN TimeLog t ON p.project_id = t.project_id)"
  );
}

}  // namespace project_time_tracking
```</answer>
